coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of highly fluorescent hydrophobic carbon dots by hot injection method using Paraplast as precursor
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Talib, Abou
Pandey, Sunil
Thakur, Mukeshchand
Wu, Hui-Fen
coredata.dc:description: We have reported synthesis of bright blue colored hydrophobic carbon dots (hC-dots) using highly pure blend of polymers called Paraplast. We developed a hot injection method for making nearly monodispersed hC-dots with a diameter in a range: 5–30nm as confirmed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The involvement of various functional groups was confirmed by Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR) spectroscopy. These hC-dots were incubated with breast cancer stem cells in order to check the entry as well as biological imaging. The cells were analyzed using epifluorescent microscopy. hC-dots showed concentration dependent cytotoxicity (LD50: 50mg/ml) and could be used for bioimaging even at lower concentration (0.5mg/ml). hC-dots were found to be versatile agents for peeping inside the cells which could also be used for delivery of water insoluble chemotherapeutic agents to variety of solid tumors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrophobic
Carbon dots
Paraplast
Breast cancer stem cells
Biological imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis and (b) PL spectra of hC-dots explaining their characteristic optical properties. The inset of (a) shows bright blue colored hC-dots under the influence of UV light (λem=365nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) HRTEM image of hC-dots depicting high monodispersity under the considered area of microscopy. (b) Histogram showing the ranges of hC-dots in terms of size and their abundance in the solution (under the observed area during microscopy).
(a) Raman Spectra and (b) X-ray Diffraction (XRD) of hC-dots exhibiting their signature properties.
FT-IR spectra of synthesized carbon dots using KBr pelleting method.

Fluorescent imaging of BCSCs in presence of hC-dots. Control group showing cells (a, b, c) labeling using commercially available blue fluorescent CdSe quantum dots. Lower panel (d, e, f) shows cell labeling using synthesized hC-dots (scale bar: 100μm).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of highly fluorescent hydrophobic carbon dots by hot injection method using Paraplast as precursor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Doctoral Degree Program in Marine Biotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medical Science and Technology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung, 80424, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Medical Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have reported synthesis of bright blue colored hydrophobic carbon dots (hC-dots) using highly pure blend of polymers called Paraplast. We developed a hot injection method for making nearly monodispersed hC-dots with a diameter in a range: 5–30nm as confirmed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The involvement of various functional groups was confirmed by Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR) spectroscopy. These hC-dots were incubated with breast cancer stem cells in order to check the entry as well as biological imaging. The cells were analyzed using epifluorescent microscopy. hC-dots showed concentration dependent cytotoxicity (LD50: 50mg/ml) and could be used for bioimaging even at lower concentration (0.5mg/ml). hC-dots were found to be versatile agents for peeping inside the cells which could also be used for delivery of water insoluble chemotherapeutic agents to variety of solid tumors.

ce:section.ce:para: Designing novel protocols of synthesis of hydrophilic as well as hydrophobic carbon dots (C-dots) has proved to be indispensible of consortium of applications such as biological sensing, drug delivery and other clinical applications [1]. There are very synthetic and natural procedures for the synthesis of C-dots using variety of precursors. Some of the methods which have been widely used for C-dot synthesis are microwave assisted synthesis [2–4], laser ablation of graphite [5,6], thermal cracking of organic compounds [7,8], electro-oxidation of graphite [9], and oxidation of candle soot [10].

Fluorescent properties of the dots can be tuned by different surface modification such as C-dots functionalized with octadecylamine exhibits blue florescence [11] while those orchestrated with diamond shows red color [12]. There are very few reports on synthesis of C-dots using natural plant materials as precursors. Recently, C-dot was synthesized using orange juice [13], jaggery, bread and sugar [14]. These C-dots being made from natural materials become exceptionally biocompatible and cost effective for bulk production.

In the present work, we are reporting a facile synthesis of hydrophobic carbon dots (hC-dots) using highly pristine Paraplast, a polymer based material used in tissue embedding and making prosthesis devices. Paraplast being a very pure polymer composite got transformed to extremely fluorescent C-dots having ideal optical properties with bright blue fluorescence. Due to the hot injection method used for the synthesis of hC-dots, the particles were found to be monodisperse in the solution which may lead to uniform optical properties of carbon dots. Intense fluorescence of hC-dots was exploited for biological imaging of cancer stem cells which often reinitiate the cancer development after the surgery or chemotherapy. Due to hydrophobic nature, the entry of hC-dots was facilitated to cancer stems cells after observation using fluorescence microscopy. This property can be used for delivery of water insoluble drugs to solid tumors which remains an intimidating task due to the relative hydrophobicity of the anti-neoplastic drugs.

Paraplast was purchased from Leica, Germany. The solvents used for the experiments were of analytical grade. All the experiments were performed using ultra-pure deionized water (18MU Milli-Q water purification system, Millipore Inc., Bedford, MA, USA). As received cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) (Gibco®, USA) supplemented with fetal bovine serum (8.5%), insulin (10μU/ml) and hydrocortisone (1μM) were all procured from Lonza Inc. USA.

Three grams of Paraplast granules was allowed to melt at 70°C using temperature controlled hot plate. This melt was rapidly injected in nitrogen flushed hot solution of toluene (Sigma, USA) followed by a gentle agitation for 3h. To enhance the passivation as well as fluorescence properties of hC-dots, 0.4ml of dodecanethiol (Sigma, USA) was added during the onset of stirring. The solution turned to yellowish green after completion of the reaction. This solution was ultra-filtered using 0.2μm filter to avoid residual impurities which may interfere in the results and may interfere in the quantification of the analyte under consideration.

Breast cancer stem cells (BCSCs) were considered for fluorescent imaging using hC-dots. The cells (density: 3×104 cells/well in a 96 well-plate) were cultured in DMEM medium (8.5% fetal bovine serum and 0.5% penicillin–streptomycin mixture) at the standard conditions of 5% CO2 at 37°C. Highly pure hC-dots (0.5mg/ml) was added at kept for incubation for 6h and after 24h. Labeled cells were imaged under epifluorescence microscope.

UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy of hC-dots were performed using UV-Visible Spectrophotometer (Thermo, Evolution USA) and fluorescence spectroscopy (Hitachi F 2007, Japan) respectively. To elucidate the size and shape of hC-dots, High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) (Jeol, Japan) was used. The purified hC-dots were dried on carbon coated copper grid for electron microscopic examination. Crystallinity of hC-dots was comprehended using X-ray Diffraction (XRD) (Phillips, The Netherlands). For analysis, samples were coated on glass coverslip and dried under ambient conditions. Raman spectra of hC-dots were studied using Jobin-Yvon Labram spectrometer. Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR) spectroscopy (Brucker, Germany) studies were performed within the spectral window of 500 to 2750cm−1. Biological imaging of BCSCs was performed using epifluorescent microscope (ESPA Systems Co. Ltd., Taiwan).

After rapid injection of the Paraplast melt to toluene solution, the color of the solution changed to brownish yellow which is a preliminary indication of C-dot synthesis in the solution [15]. This solution displayed a prominent blue color after irradiation with UV light (λ=365nm). Growth of bright blue fluorescence in C-dots is due to the recombination of electron-hole pair from defects in graphene sheets which act as an initial parent material synthesis of C-dots [4]. These defects may be due to oxygenation of graphene sheets or due to surface decoration with functional moieties such as –COOH and –NH2 functional groups. However, the concrete modus operandi of this phenomenon is still oblivious to the scientific world.

UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 1a) of the solution displayed a peak at 285nm indicating the formation of hC-dots [16]. The cause of intense UV peaks is due to π electron transitions in C-dots containing oxygen as functional groups as per our earlier discussion. Absorbance is due to π→π* electron transition of CC and to n→π* of carbonyl groups [17]. PL studies show excitation dependent emission by C-dots as shown in Fig. 1b. These carbon having different states of hybridization (Sp2 and Sp3) generation mini Sp2–Sp3 islands which is responsible for its inherent fluorescence.

HRTEM image (Fig. 2a) displays synthesis of spherical C-dots of maximum average size of 20nm. Fig. 2b displays the size distribution of hC-dots which further shows dominance of 20nm particles and others in a range of 5–30nm in the selected area for electron microscopy. Sharon and co-workers showed optimum size for theranostic applications to be in size range of 5–10nm [2,3,16]. However, Liu et al. have also shown 5nm and 20nm C-dots for bioimaging applications [17]. Our size ranges from 5 to 30nm which could be used for future therapeutic delivery as well as diagnostic applications. Raman spectra (Fig. 3a) of PA derived C-dots show G and D-bands. G-band observed at 1596cm−1 with respect to more intense peak of D-band at 521cm−1 indicates the presence of amorphous carbon nanomaterials in the form of C-dots [18]. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 3b) displays characteristic peaks for the diffraction patterns of graphitic carbon. Peaks at 2θ=~20° and a wide feeble peak at 2θ=~40° which are assigned to (002) and (101) respectively [19].

Functional groups on the hC-dots were studied using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Narrow IR band at 676cm−1 depicts CH2 bends and band at 1465cm−1 relates to O–H group functionalization of the nanoparticles. Bands at 1550 and 1655cm−1 correspond to N–H bending of primary amine and amide C–O stretching vibrations. The presence of carboxylate functional moieties is responsible for creating energy traps [6] thus making the C-dots highly fluorescent (Fig. 4 ).

Intense fluorescent properties of C-dots have made them ideal nanomaterials for biological imaging [20]. Carbon nanoparticles are proved to be superior materials over conventional nanoparticles such as gold due to high background scattering from biological cells [21] as well as least toxicity. Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have constrained applications in imaging due to their hostile impact on biological system [22]. In Fig. 5 , we have exploited hC-dots for biological imaging of breast cancer stem cells. Control groups were BCSCs treated with commercial CdSe quantum dots showing bright blue colored fluorescence (Fig. 5a–c). Comparable fluorescence imaging was obtained for synthesized hC-dots with semiconductor quantum dots (Fig. 5d–f). There was no significant difference in the bioimaging property of hC-dots after 6h (Fig. 5f) and 24h (Fig. S2c) incubation with cells. The C-dots being hydrophobic in nature can traverse the cells membrane more easily than hydrophilic C-dots. This property makes the present C-dots highly suitable for biological imaging.

At a concentration of 0.5mg/ml even after 24h, there was 98.1% cell viability (Fig. S1) and fluorescence was retained in the cells (Fig. S2). In tumor milieu, stem cells are considered to be real delinquents in reoccurrence of variety of cancers [23]. They also confer drug resistance to cancer cells which becomes major obstacle for chemotherapy [24].

Synthesized hC-dots are expected to reside in cytoplasm of BCSCs. Mechanism of entry of carbon based nanomaterials even C-dots inside cells follow standard routes such as receptor-mediated, clathrin-mediated or caveolae-mediated endocytosis [25,26]. Wu et al. discuss the comparative behavior of size dependent localization of carbon based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) inside cells in the cytoplasm [26]. Similar results were obtained Yuan et al. were they studied role of different functional groups loaded GQDs on cancer cell lines and shown cytoplasmic localization but not nucleus [27]. Sahu et al. also showed localization of green synthesized C-dots in cytoplasm whereas they were forbidden from nucleus [13]. Therefore, entry and cytoplasmic localization of C-dots in stem cells can lay down the foundations of novel materials for targeting stem cells.

Paraplast was proved to be an excellent precursor for the synthesis of hC-dots using the hot injection method. This resulted in synthesis of bright blue colored hC-dots when observed under intense UV light. The size of the hC-dots were found to be in the range of 5–30nm with predominance of ~20nm sized nanoparticles in the solution. High fluorescence intensity of hC-dots was proved to be of immense importance in biological imaging of cancer stem cells as observed using fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2014.11.058.
